# Chapter 10

[q4m9t1z8]The message about the race came as a voice burst in Flynn’s ear while he was under the Shelby with Diesel, heat pressed down on the tin floor like a hand. It wasn’t his choice. The implant saw a keyword, pushed it through. Fifty miles. No rules. A start time that was a lie and a place that was just coordinates.

[b7k2r5x0]Cage didn’t look up when Flynn told him. He lay on his back in the shade of the carport, hands black to the wrists, turning a spark plug between finger and thumb like a coin. “Good,” he said. Like someone had brought water. Like it was owed.

[m1v6c8p3]Diesel’s face stayed flat. She tightened a hose clamp until the screwdriver bit slipped and rasped metal. “We don’t need it,” she said. “We already know she runs.”

[h0s8d2n5]Cage smiled without showing teeth. “We need to see her against other men,” he said. “We need to feel what the road does when it knows it’s being asked.” He said it like the road was alive and could be coaxed or punished.

[r9a1w4j6]Flynn listened and felt his stomach climb. He’d driven the rat-rod hard enough to sweat through his shirt. He’d driven the Shelby careful, hands light, Diesel watching gauges. A race was different. A race wasn’t practice. It was a crowd and a story and a pressure you couldn’t back out of without becoming nothing.

[n2f7l0q9]Birdie heard about it and came running from the container where he slept, barefoot in the dust, a wrench hanging from his pocket like jewelry. “Where,” he said. “Who’s going.” His eyes were wide and bright as if someone had promised him a fire.

[vt5c3y1d]Wrench was sitting on an upturned tire, cigarette dead between his fingers, watching nothing. When Cage said the coordinates, Wrench nodded once like he’d been expecting it. “People’ll be there,” he said. “All kinds.”

[k8u0e2g7]They left late afternoon, when the sun had started to turn the sky a harsh white instead of blue. Two vehicles. The Shelby on its own wheels, low and clean among the rust. Flynn in the rat-rod behind it, engine loud and uneven, smelling of old fuel and oil that had seen too many fires.

[z3n6p9w1]The highway they took was an older one, cracked concrete with weeds growing in seams, lanes widened by time and heat. The pods didn’t come out here. Their maps pretended this road didn’t exist. That was why it existed.

[a5d1k8s2]Flynn held the rat-rod steady at ninety, keeping distance. The Shelby ahead looked like a piece of the past that had refused to die, its lines sharp even under dust. Cage drove it with one hand, elbow out, like he was bored. The sight made Flynn angry and thrilled at the same time.

[j6p0h4c9]They found the meet at a dead interchange where two highways crossed without touching, ramps to nowhere, the skeleton of a plan abandoned when the money moved to the arcologies. Under the overpass, people had made a kind of market. Fires in drums. Tables of parts. Men and women with their faces lit orange, speaking low.

[p0e7n3v2]Flynn shut the rat-rod down and the sudden quiet hit him like pressure. He could hear his own blood and the far wind under the bridge. Somewhere a generator droned. Someone laughed and it sounded wrong, too light, like it belonged in a city.

[c4r1t7m9]Cage stepped out and the crowd shifted, the way water moves around a rock. People recognized him, or recognized his kind. He didn’t greet them. He walked like the ground was his.

[y2j9f0s6]Diesel stayed close to the Shelby, eyes on the hood seam, listening to the engine cooling, the ticks and pops. She looked more tense here than during the heist. She kept her hands on the car like she could feel its pulse.

[me4t0y6p]They went to sign in at a folding table under a tarp. A kid with a tablet and a pistol on his hip took names that weren’t names. Took cash in bills that smelled like sweat. There was no bracket. No waiver. The only rule was you stop if someone goes down. That was what they said.

[v7s1n5k3]The racers gathered on the shoulder of the road at dusk. The start line was a strip of tape across cracked asphalt. Someone had planted two flares in the dirt like candles. Behind them, trucks idled with lights off, ready to haul people away if the law came. The law never came. The law was busy elsewhere.

[b2h6c0z9]Flynn climbed into the rat-rod and the seat was hot from the sun. He adjusted the belt twice, hands shaking. His implant tried to play him a calming ad—breathing exercises sponsored by some beverage company—but he killed it with a thought and felt the anger flare like fuel.

[na8q2v1m]Birdie leaned in the window. “Don’t baby it,” he said. “If you lose, Cage’ll say it was your fault.” He grinned like it was a joke. His gums were white around the teeth.

[r3p9x6c1]“Thanks,” Flynn said.

[t6k1j8f0]On the far side of the line, Cage sat in the Shelby, head turned slightly, eyes on the road ahead like it was a long tunnel. He didn’t look at Flynn. He didn’t need to. Flynn could feel him anyway, like a heat source.

[w9c4m2s7]Other cars revved. A bright orange Camaro with polished wheels. A black Charger with a hood scoop like a throat. Something European and low that didn’t belong here. And then a silver coupe with a clean body and a driver in a white shirt, sleeves rolled neat. Rich kid. His car had been built by someone else and it still had new tires.

[p5z0d7k2]Wrench walked down the line, checking faces, counting. When he got to Flynn, he paused. “If it goes bad,” he said, “don’t chase it. Let it go. Road don’t care.”

[k1q8n4v6]Flynn wanted to ask him what he meant but the flares were already burning down and the air smelled of sulfur and gasoline and the desert itself, hot stone. A woman stood between the cars with her arms raised.

[e0u3h9a7]Flynn stared at her hands. He thought about his own hands on the wheel, about the way the rat-rod vibrated like a living thing trying to shake him off. He thought about the guard in Phoenix and the sound of Cage’s fists. He tried to swallow and his throat was dry.

[zn6m1t8p]The woman dropped her arms.

[h7v2r0x9]The world snapped forward. Engines screamed. Flynn dumped the clutch and the rat-rod leapt, rear tires biting and then slipping, smoke in the mirrors. The Shelby shot ahead like it had been waiting its whole life for this, clean power, no hesitation.

[m8b5d1s3]They hit a hundred in seconds. The road narrowed where the concrete had fallen away and the dirt shoulder rose like a berm. Flynn kept his eyes on the taillights ahead, on the line of cars stretching out into dusk. The sky was turning bruised purple. The heat didn’t leave.

[f2n7a9j5]At mile ten the pack had thinned. There were three in front: Cage’s Shelby, the black Charger, and the silver coupe with the rich kid. The coupe stayed close to the Shelby, drafting like he’d watched old videos, like he believed physics would make him brave.

[ue5r8p0d]Flynn could see Cage’s brake lights flicker once, brief, not slowing, just a signal. The Charger moved left. The coupe moved right. The road ahead had a ripple where the ground had shifted, a long crack filled with sand.

[k4s1g7m2]Flynn felt it before he understood it. Cage had set the line. He’d put the others where the road would punish them. The Shelby stayed centered, stable. The coupe hit the ripple at speed and its rear stepped out.

[x6d9n2b0]For a second the coupe corrected, tires squealing, a clean countersteer like a training sim. Then the rear came back harder and the car went sideways across the lane. Headlights flashed across the desert like a search beam, catching a stand of dead scrub.

[j8p3t0v7]The Charger clipped the coupe’s rear quarter. Metal rang. Sparks flew in a long arc. The coupe spun, hit the berm, launched. It was airborne, silent for an instant, a bright silver fish above black land.

[q9h1c6s4]Then it came down and the sound was not a crash but a series of impacts, like a giant beating a drum. The car rolled. Panels tore off. Glass became dust. Fire bloomed and was snatched sideways by the wind.

[a0m7w2k9]Flynn lifted off the throttle so hard his chest slammed the belt. The rat-rod shook as he braked. He saw Cage ahead keep going for another two seconds before his brake lights finally flared. Like he was considering whether stopping was worth it.

[pt3e8n1y]Cars behind were already slowing. The code of the road. They pulled onto the shoulder, hazards blinking, the modern courtesy lights like little lies. Flynn rolled to a stop with the coupe burning fifty yards ahead and smoke pouring into the twilight.

[v1k6d0r8]He got out and his legs felt wrong, too light. The heat from the fire hit him even at distance, a wave. He could hear someone screaming and he knew it was human before he could see the body. The sound was too raw for anything else.

[s4c2m8j0]People ran toward the wreck. Somebody had a fire extinguisher that looked pathetic against the flames. Flynn moved with them, feet sinking into sand, heart beating so hard he could taste iron.

[n9p5u1x4]The coupe had split open. The roof was crushed. The engine bay was a furnace. The driver was half out of the seat, pinned, his white shirt turned gray and pink. One of his arms was wrong, bent where it shouldn’t bend. His face was a mask of blood and dust and shock.

[g6t0w9c3]“Don’t touch me,” he screamed, then “Touch me,” then “Please.” His implant was trying to talk over him, pushing automated triage instructions in a calm voice that kept getting drowned by the screaming.

[h2q7v3m9]Flynn saw bone. He saw skin peeled back like meat. He saw the driver’s eyes tracking, not understanding. The man looked young, maybe twenty-two, maybe thirty, rich enough to keep his hands soft. He looked at Flynn as if Flynn could pay for this to stop.

[r0n4k8d2]Cage arrived last, walking, not running. He stood at the edge of the circle and watched. His face was still. In the firelight his scars looked deeper, cut into him by old decisions.

[w2m9s6f1]Diesel pushed through and dropped to her knees beside the driver, hands steady. She pulled a small kit from her pocket, slapped a patch on his neck, an emergency transmitter. “You’re going to breathe,” she said, voice low. “Hear me. You’re going to breathe.”

[c7p1t5z8]The driver tried to nod and couldn’t. His jaw clicked. He made a sound like a dog hit by a car. Flynn’s stomach twisted. He wanted to turn away and couldn’t.

[y8v0q2l6]The fire caught the upholstery and the smell changed, sweet and chemical. Someone finally got the extinguisher to foam the engine bay. White spray turned black instantly. The driver’s screams rose and broke.

[p8d2h7r1]One of them looked at Cage and then away, like he knew who he was and didn’t want to be seen knowing. They cut the driver free with a tool that hissed and spit sparks. The driver’s leg came loose and flopped, and the scream he made was animal.

[f9w1c4j7]The medics slid the driver onto a board. A patch on his chest blinked red and then green as it found a signal. “You’re going to be revived,” one of them said, like it was a comfort. “You’re going to come back.”

[qp6t2n8x]“No,” the driver said. It was the first word that wasn’t a scream. “No. Don’t. Don’t.”

[d8r3y1k0]The medic didn’t react. He jabbed a needle into the man’s arm. Sedative. The scream softened, went muffled. The man’s eyes stayed open, wet and furious.

[v0s7p5m1]A second board came out with straps and a hood that looked like a plastic bag. Flynn watched the medics seal it around the driver’s head. It wasn’t suffocation. It was oxygen and sensor gel and a thing meant to keep the brain from dying too far.

[kt1h9c3w]Diesel stood up, hands shaking now that she wasn’t touching him. She wiped her palms on her pants and the blood left a dark smear. She looked at Flynn and he saw something like apology in her eyes. Like she’d brought him to a church and shown him the god.

[z7c0m2u8]Cage spoke finally. “He’ll be fine,” he said. Not to the driver. To no one. To the night itself.

[n1j8r6p4]Flynn stared at him. “Fine,” he said, voice cracking. “You call that fine.”

[x3e6t0v9]Cage’s eyes slid to Flynn like a knife turning. “He wanted it,” he said. “He paid for it. He came out here. Same as you.”

[m0p4k7s2]Flynn’s hands were trembling and he couldn’t stop them. He climbed back into the rat-rod and sat there, staring at the cracked dash. His implant tried to offer him grief counseling. A cheery voice said help was available. He laughed once, short, like a cough.

[s6t9w0c1]The race restarted because there was nothing else to do. The flares were replaced. The remaining cars lined up. Cage went back to the Shelby. The Charger driver’s face was pale behind the windshield, but he stayed. No one wanted to be the one who admitted fear.

[b0n5x2j8]Flynn didn’t line up. He stayed on the shoulder with Diesel and Wrench, engines idling, watching Cage pull into position like the wreck was a minor delay. Wrench’s cigarette shook between his fingers.

[g8r1p6m3]“That kid,” Wrench said. “Name’s Mateo. Works at a finance desk in Phoenix. Comes out here twice a month. Thinks it makes him a man.”

[u1m8k3q6]Diesel didn’t look at Flynn. “Revival don’t fix everything,” she said. “People come back wrong. Nerves burn. Pain pathways get stuck. Sometimes the implant keeps the memory too sharp. They live in it.”

[t9v3s0h2]Flynn swallowed. “Does Cage know that.”

[xe2m7k1p]Diesel finally met his eyes. “He knows,” she said. “That’s why he likes it.”

[c1s6n8w0]The second start went off. The Shelby leapt ahead and disappeared into darkness, taillights shrinking. The sound of it was clean and hungry. The others followed, their engines uneven, roaring into the night like animals released.

[j0k5p2r9]Flynn watched the road until the lights were gone. The desert took the noise and held it far away. He imagined the Shelby running alone now, Cage smiling to himself, the speed a prayer. He imagined Mateo in a sealed bag, eyes open, being carried toward a machine that would force him back.

[v6y1d8m4]When Cage returned an hour later, he was grinning. Dust on his teeth. He climbed out and slapped the Shelby’s roof like it was a dog. People came to him, clapped his shoulders, offered bottles. He took them like tribute.

[k9p0t6a1]Flynn stayed back. Cage saw him and walked over. Up close Flynn could smell the gasoline on him, the sweat. Cage’s eyes were bright. He looked alive in a way Flynn had never seen in anyone else.

[e7m2s9d0]“You should’ve run,” Cage said.

[r6c1w8n3]Flynn shook his head. “There was a man,” he said.

[q5t8h0j7]Cage’s smile thinned. “There was a race,” he answered. “You can’t have the thing without the cost. That’s what makes it the thing.”

[t2d9m4x0]Flynn wanted to tell him he didn’t want it anymore. He wanted to tell him he’d been wrong. But the words wouldn’t line up. Cage stood there waiting, calm, like he’d already won.

[p7m1k6v8]On the drive back to the Compound, Flynn stayed behind the Shelby, headlights fixed on the dust plume. The road looked the same as it always had—cracked, empty, indifferent. But now he knew what it could do to a body at speed, how fast metal became knives.

[w1z8c2m5]He kept seeing Mateo’s eyes. Not the screaming. The look after the sedative, when the scream had stopped but the fear hadn’t. That look stayed, because the implant would keep it, because the machine would bring him back and the memory would still be there.

[n7k3p0s2]Cage’s taillights ahead were steady, unwavering. Flynn followed them because that was what he did now. Because he didn’t know how to leave. Because the only honest thing left might be the road and the consequences, and he had just seen what honesty looked like.
